title: Black Death arrives in Europe
summary: In the autumn of 1347 Genoese galleys carrying survivors of an outbreak in the Crimea put in at the Sicilian port of Messina. Within four years the pandemic later called the Black Death had swept across the Mediterranean, north into England and the Baltic, east to Russia, and south into North Africa and the Near East. It killed perhaps a third to a half of the population of the affected regions, ended the demographic expansion of the high Middle Ages, and rewrote the balance between labour and land, peasant and lord, Christian and Muslim, over most of the fourteenth-century world.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The pathogen behind the Black Death was Yersinia pestis, a bacterium that lives in the digestive tracts of certain fleas and infects small rodents. Under normal conditions it circulates quietly in enzootic reservoirs, most notably among wild marmots and gerbils in Inner Asia. When environmental stress or animal mortality forces its fleas onto humans, it produces three overlapping diseases: bubonic plague, marked by swollen lymph nodes called buboes; septicaemic plague, in which the bacteria overwhelm the bloodstream; and pneumonic plague, spread by droplet from person to person and almost invariably fatal within days.

Recent genetic work on skeletons from mass graves in London, Barcelona, and elsewhere has confirmed that the fourteenth-century pandemic was caused by a strain of Y. pestis very similar to those still circulating in Central Asia. Historical and paleoclimate evidence points to an origin somewhere in the Tian Shan or Mongolian steppes in the 1330s or 1340s, most likely spread along the caravan and postal networks of the Mongol Empire, which by then reached from the Pacific to the Black Sea.

The first documented outbreaks in the western sources are in Central Asia and the Golden Horde in the mid-1340s. In 1345 to 1346 an epidemic ravaged the Mongol army of Khan Janibek, which was besieging the Genoese trading colony of Caffa on the Crimean peninsula. According to the Italian notary Gabriele de' Mussi, who was writing from Piacenza a few years later, the Mongols catapulted plague-ridden corpses over the walls of Caffa in a grim early experiment in biological warfare. The Genoese survivors escaped by sea in the autumn of 1347, carrying rats, fleas, and infection with them.

Europe in the mid-fourteenth century was ripe for disaster. Population had grown steadily for two centuries and, in many regions, had reached or exceeded what medieval agriculture could reliably feed. The Great Famine of 1315 to 1317 had already killed millions; the Hundred Years' War, begun between France and England in 1337, was disrupting trade and grain supply. Malnutrition weakened populations across the continent. Sanitation in most towns was poor. And the very trade networks that had brought Chinese silk, Indian pepper, and Baltic fur to Genoese and Venetian warehouses now brought a much more efficient parasite.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The first western European outbreak began at the Sicilian port of Messina in October 1347, when Genoese galleys from Caffa put in for supplies. The Franciscan chronicler Michele da Piazza described sailors who died within days of arrival, buboes bursting through their skin, and how the disease jumped to the local population and then to the countryside. By January 1348 it had reached Genoa itself, Marseille, and the Dalmatian coast; by spring the ports of Sicily were emptying. Ships driven from Genoa reached Pisa and then Florence. In March 1348 the plague was general in Tuscany.

Giovanni Boccaccio, whose Decameron opens with a description of the Florentine plague of 1348, records symptoms with clinical precision. Some patients showed the characteristic groin and armpit buboes; others succumbed within hours to a haemorrhagic pneumonia in which they coughed blood and died. Grave-diggers were themselves buried. Whole families died in shuttered houses; parents abandoned children; priests refused to give last rites. Florence, whose pre-plague population may have exceeded 100,000, lost more than half its people in a few months.

By the summer of 1348 the pandemic had leapt the Alps and moved north through France. Marseille, Avignon, Lyon, Paris. At Avignon, seat of the papacy under Clement VI, the pope's own physician Guy de Chauliac survived the disease and left the fullest medical account of it. Clement's advisers set two fires burning at either end of his private chamber and kept him between them, on the grounds that plague was carried by miasmas, corrupt air; whether or not the treatment helped, Clement lived. He issued bulls protecting Jews accused across the Rhineland of poisoning wells, a persistent slander that produced pogroms across the Empire from Strasbourg to Basel.

England was infected by June 1348, first at the little port of Melcombe in Dorset. The disease reached London by autumn and by mid-1349 had swept the whole kingdom; in Ireland, Wales, and Scotland it followed within months. Contemporary chroniclers agree that a very large proportion of the population died. The Winchester diocesan register shows the bishop appointing new incumbents to some 40 percent of vacant parishes within a year. Modern estimates for the whole of Europe put mortality at between 30 and 50 percent between 1347 and 1352.

The pandemic ran no less devastatingly across the Muslim Mediterranean. The Egyptian historian al-Maqrizi and the Andalusi physician Ibn al-Khatib wrote long accounts of the plague in Cairo, Alexandria, Damascus, and Granada. Ibn al-Khatib, in a treatise refuting the doctrine that plague could not be contagious because such contagion would contradict a hadith of the Prophet, wrote that observation and reason showed clear evidence of person-to-person transmission. Egypt lost perhaps a third of its population by 1349; the Mamluk state, which depended on Circassian slave soldiers imported through the Black Sea, was permanently weakened. Ibn Battuta, travelling home to Morocco, met the pandemic in Damascus and later in his own family in Tangier.

By 1353 the first great wave had passed. Successive smaller epidemics returned to European and Mediterranean cities roughly every ten to fifteen years for the rest of the fourteenth and much of the fifteenth centuries, and, less regularly, until the eighteenth. The word plague entered the everyday vocabulary of premodern life.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The demographic collapse was too large to hide. Whole villages in northern France, southern England, and southern Germany were abandoned. Cistercian abbey rentals for the 1350s often show a third or more of the tenancies vacant. Some estimates suggest that the population of Europe did not return to its 1340s level until the sixteenth century.

The economic consequences were, paradoxically, in many cases favourable to the survivors. With so few labourers left, wages rose sharply. In England the government of Edward III responded with the Ordinance of Labourers in 1349 and the Statute of Labourers in 1351, freezing wages at their pre-plague levels and forbidding workers to leave their villages. Similar sumptuary and wage laws followed in France, Castile, Aragon, and the Low Countries. Almost universally they failed to hold. Real wages for building workers in England roughly doubled between 1340 and 1400. Peasant families acquired more land per household, ate more meat, and married younger. In the long run the balance between labour and land shifted decisively in favour of labour, and the manorial system of unfree villein tenure was hollowed out in much of western Europe.

Social and political consequences were less benign. Peasant revolts, from the Jacquerie in France in 1358 to the Ciompi rising in Florence in 1378 and the English Peasants' Revolt of 1381, drew energy from wage laws and heavy new taxes. The papacy in Avignon, discredited both by its failures during the plague and by internal politics, suffered the Great Schism of 1378 in which rival popes reigned at Rome and Avignon for four decades. In the Muslim world the Mamluk sultanate lost the demographic base that had underwritten its military supremacy and never fully recovered; the Nasrid emirate of Granada was similarly weakened.

Jewish communities were among the first scapegoats. Between 1348 and 1350 pogroms swept through Provence, Catalonia, the Rhineland, Franconia, and Silesia. In Basel the entire Jewish community was burned in a house on an island in the Rhine on 9 January 1349; in Strasbourg some 2,000 Jews were killed a month later. The surviving communities of central and eastern Europe were pushed further east, into Poland-Lithuania, where new privileges of protection created the demographic foundations of Ashkenazi Jewry for the next four centuries.

Medical and public-health thinking began to change, if slowly. The Venetian and Ragusan authorities imposed the first quarantines in the 1370s, requiring incoming ships to wait for forty days, quaranta giorni, before landing. Milan under the Visconti pioneered the practice of walling up plague-stricken houses. Public health boards emerged in Italian cities. None of this could stop plague, but it laid the groundwork for the sanitary regimes of later centuries.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The Black Death has become the archetype of pandemic disaster in the western historical imagination. It is invoked whenever a new outbreak, from the great cholera epidemics of the nineteenth century to influenza in 1918 and COVID-19 in 2020, prompts fear on a continental scale. Historians debate almost every aspect of its legacy, from the precise mortality rate to whether it accelerated the Renaissance or delayed it, whether it strengthened or weakened Christian institutions, whether it hurt or helped the rise of centralised monarchies.

What is clear is that the pandemic ended the demographic and, in many places, the economic pattern of the high Middle Ages. The relative bargaining power of ordinary labourers rose. Marginal farmland was abandoned; the great forests of central Europe re-advanced across former fields. Written wills and estate records survive from the plague years in unusual numbers because so many people were making them at once; historians have used them to reconstruct the medieval household in previously impossible detail.

In art and religion the plague left conspicuous traces. The Danse Macabre, the Triumph of Death, the memento mori tomb sculpture of northern Europe, the flagellant processions and the cults of St Sebastian and St Roch as protectors from plague, all belong to the world after 1347. In Muslim lands scholars debated the meaning of the disaster, some seeing it as divine punishment, others, like Ibn al-Khatib in Granada, insisting on natural cause and contagion.

Modern genetic and paleogenetic research since 2011 has decisively confirmed Y. pestis as the pathogen, ended a century of academic argument about other candidates, and traced the pandemic's genetic ancestry to an outbreak in the region of Kyrgyzstan in the 1330s. The bacterium is still with us, endemic in wild rodent populations from the American West to Madagascar. What has changed is the ability to recognise, quarantine, and, since the mid-twentieth century, treat with antibiotics. The Black Death of the fourteenth century is a reminder both of what a novel epidemic can do to an unprepared society and of how much has been learned since.

worldContext.text: In northern India the Delhi Sultanate under Muhammad bin Tughluq was disintegrating from the strain of his failed schemes: forced migration of the capital, token-copper coinage, and unaffordable campaigns. In 1347 the Bahmani Sultanate broke away in the Deccan under Alauddin Bahman Shah. The plague appears to have reached Indian ports through Persian Gulf shipping, though evidence is thinner than for the Mediterranean.

The Mexica were still a minor tributary power around their new island city of Tenochtitlan under Azcapotzalco's hegemony; the great expansion under Itzcoatl and the Aztec Triple Alliance lay in the coming century. In the Andes the Inca dynasty was a local kingdom at Cuzco. Along the Mississippi Cahokia's decline was well advanced, and in the Southwest ancestral Pueblo people had regrouped in the Rio Grande and Little Colorado valleys. Old and New World disease pools were still isolated.

The Mali Empire ruled the western Sudan under Mansa Sulayman, brother of the famous Musa; Ibn Battuta would visit his court in 1352 to 1353 and leave the fullest surviving account of the empire. Along the Swahili coast Kilwa continued as a major Indian Ocean trading city. In the Ethiopian highlands the Solomonic dynasty ruled under Amda Seyon and his successors. North African cities from Fez to Cairo were hit hard by the plague.

In Aotearoa Maori communities were adapting to a temperate climate and driving the flightless moa toward extinction. On Rapa Nui the great period of moai construction continued, though environmental pressures on the island were mounting. Polynesian networks connected the islands of the eastern Pacific. Aboriginal Australians maintained the managed-burning regimes that shaped the continent's landscapes. None of these societies is known to have been reached by Y. pestis.

The Mongol Yuan dynasty of China under Toghon Temur Khan was in visible decay: repeated Yellow River floods, hyperinflation of paper currency, and, from 1351 onward, the Red Turban rebellions. Some historians read Chinese epidemic records of the 1330s and 1340s as evidence that plague was already circulating in Chinese cities; the point is debated. Japan under the divided imperial courts of the Nanboku-cho period was fighting its own civil war.

figures.name: Pope Clement VI
figures.role: Pope at Avignon during the pandemic; issued bulls protecting Jews from pogroms and consecrated the Rhone as a mass burial river

figures.name: Giovanni Boccaccio
figures.role: Florentine writer whose Decameron opens with a first-hand description of the 1348 plague in Florence

figures.name: Ibn al-Khatib
figures.role: Andalusi physician, vizier, and polymath in Granada whose treatise argued from observation for the contagious nature of plague

figures.name: Guy de Chauliac
figures.role: Papal physician at Avignon who survived the disease and wrote the leading medical account in his surgical treatise Chirurgia magna

figures.name: Al-Maqrizi
figures.role: Egyptian historian, writing a generation later, whose chronicle preserves the fullest account of the plague in Mamluk Egypt

images.caption: The Triumph of Death, oil on panel by Pieter Bruegel the Elder, c. 1562, an inheritor of the pictorial tradition born from the Black Death. Museo del Prado, Madrid.
images.credit: Pieter Bruegel the Elder (c. 1525 to 1569), via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: Burning of Jews during the Black Death, from a 14th-century manuscript of the Antiquitates Flandriae. Royal Library of Belgium, Brussels.
images.credit: Anonymous 14th-century Flemish illuminator, via Wikimedia Commons
